So I think this implementation of seek will be a bit more tricky than the other ones. Mainly because the `ReadFileChunk` class has an additional abstraction that controls where you start and limits how far you can read into the file. Using the `self._start_byte` for the beginning of the file and `self._size` for the end of the file, this class ensures you cannot read before the beginning or after the end. So I think that passing in `whence` of non-zero breaks this abstraction. To fix it, I think it would be reasonable to:

1) Calculate the `where` value that is passed into `self._fileobj.seek()` based on the value of `whence` passed in along with the `self._start_byte` and `self._size`. So for `0`, where would be `self._start_byte + where`. For `1`, `self._amount_read + where`. For `2`, `self._size + where`.

2) Always pass `0` for `whence` into `self._fileobj.seek()`

3) Use the same calculated value in the progress callback and when setting `self._amount_read`

I think it is actually better to have `levels` adaptive. The function generates a `levels x levels` output matrix (for each distance and each angle). If the maximum value << `levels`, there will many zeros. An alternative would be to use sparse matrices. In any case, as a user I would not expect that the co-occurrence matrix covers the entire range defined by the data type unless I wish to combine the co-occurrence matrices of many images, which seems a bit exotic as an application (and which is still feasible, even if `levels` is adaptive. 

But if there is a strong incentive to have the matrices cover the entire data range, I can of course change this accordingly. Using the max just seems more logical to me.

The first iteration of the loop will be `i=0`. I prefer `++i` over `i++`. They have the same effect if they are used alone. When they are used as part of a more complex statement then `++i` will increment and evaluate to the incremented value while `i++` will increment the variable but evaluate to the pre-incremented value.

So I like switching to `i < segment.size()` (I'd prefer spaces around the comparison operator though), don't have a strong opinion on the `++i` vs `i++` but regardless the comment needs to go.

Possibly the whole thing means `session id serialized` (the last `s` standing for `serialized`), as the `sid_s` value is unserialized to produce the actual session.
It is, however, _not_ the session's id and naming the field `session_id` would be confusing (looking through the code you'd then see that the value stored here as `session_id` is referred to as `sid_s` in `flask_kvsession`, and said value gets unserialized to produce the session object which contains the session id).

The field contains the session's key value as stored in `flask_kvsession`'s sessionstore.
I've named this field `sid_s` so that it corresponds with the naming used in `flask_kvsession` to avoid confusion.

Another approach that would be a bit more bulletproof, would be to request a `Snapshot` for the `PathGlobs` represented by the option, and to then recompute that `Snapshot` after _every_ file invalidation event (and it would need to be every event, because the `Snapshot` does not tell you which symlinks it traversed... it just guarantees that it does), to determine whether the `Snapshot`'s digest had changed. If the digest hadn't changed, this would take a few milliseconds... if it had changed, it would take a bit longer, but not much.

It may not be 100% general though, because if any of the globs represent symlinks outside of the working copy, we'd fail to cover them. Perhaps that requirement is fine?

This test is quite long, and I think it would be beneficial to break it up a little bit.
e.x. If `go@globusid.org` has changed and returns different data, we should still correctly test that sending `usernames` and `ids` results in an error.

We don't necessarily need to be very strict about where/how we break things down. Under the most rigorous regimen, we'd have a distinct test for each behavior of each call, with each set of parameters we give it -- seems a bit like overkill, IMHO.

However, I think it's useful, especially with a test like this which is vulnerable to breakage due to external changes, to separate testing of error conditions from working features.

I'd also be curious about @corpulentcoffee's and @jaswilli's opinions on this matter -- where and how they like to have unit tests broken apart.

Class of future use cases, which this facilitates, are characterized by the need to determine and persist the exact migration context, whereby "exact migration context" is constituted by a hash of `migrate_version` and `module_name`. Only if those are not `None`, this promise can be kept, but I see no other way to strengthen the promise further, so user has to take care of this.

Basically, this enables tagging records or values or columns/fields with the hash and refer to them precisely afterwards.

Some illustrative examples:
- External ids created by migration scripts
- Reliable Field/column prefixing

Nice work. This PR is looking really good! I have just one more request: split this test method in two. unittest-compatible test runners execute test methods one at a time, in alphanumeric order. As a result, we can create a pair of methods named e.g. `test_01_uploads_langpacks` and `test_02_remove_langpacks`, and know that they will be executed in the right order.

One thing to keep in mind when doing this is that the test should still be valid even if one or more test methods fails. What happens if `test_01_...` fails? Will `test_02_...` still work correctly?

The problem with writing tests this way is that if no exception is raised, then everything looks good... so it's generally better practice to do:
```python
with pytest.raises(RuntimeError) as excinfo:
    ...
assert excinfo.value.args[0] == "..."
...
```
which will actually fail if it doesn't see a `RuntimeError.` OTOH if this is copied directly from the upstream fix and you'd rather keep it close to upstream there's a reasonable argument for that.

Dependencies are not properly tracked here. In particular, changes in `*.proto.in` files would not cause a rebuild. Also, IIUC, you are calling `gen_proto.py` for every `.proto.in` file, but `gen_proto.py` generates the same set of output files. Multiple execs of `gen_proto.py` writing to the same output files would break parallel builds.

It is better to split this `add_custom_command` into two parts: running `gen_proto.py` to generate `*.proto` from `*.proto.in`, and running `protoc` on produced `*.proto` files to get `*.pb.cc` and `*.pb.h`.  `add_custom_command` invocations need both `OUTPUT` and `DEPENDS` arguments to properly track the deps.
